$4.73

Free Cash Flow/Share

-$0.25

Seeking Alpha Quant Score

Hold: 2.70

(Source - Seeking Alpha)

The company's Rule of 40 performance is shown in the table below and has been lackluster:

Rule of 40 Performance (Unadjusted)

FQ2 2025

Revenue Growth %

5.6%

Operating Margin

6.8%

Total

12.4%

(Source - Seeking Alpha)

Capital Allocation

Over the trailing twelve months, free cash flow was $5.2 million, and capital expenditures were $2.4 million.

However, the company paid a hefty $21.8 million in stock-based compensation in the last four quarters, so free cash flow net of SBC was actually negative ($16.6 million).

The net dollar decrease in stock outstanding for the last four quarters was valued at $12 million.

SBC as a percentage of revenue over the trailing twelve-month period was 9.0%, a high figure for a mature company.

Net stock buybacks versus SBC have only been about half of the SBC it has issues, so there has been net dilution to shareholders.

While in FQ3 2024, the board announced a $50 million stock buyback authorization through August 8, 2025, only $11.5 million has been repurchased in reported quarters since then.

Management plans include the potential to use part of its $400 million credit revolver for further stock repurchases.

Why I’m Neutral On i3 Verticals

i3 has made several moves to strategically realign its business recently, with the sale of its Merchant Services segment to Payroc in September 2024 for $438 million in cash and its Healthcare Revenue Cycle Management segment to Infinx for $96 million in cash.

The firm also recently acquired a small utility billing software company to enhance its utilities offering.

The result of these divestitures and recent acquisition will be to concentrate the company's efforts on the public sector vertical going forward.

In the most recent quarter, i3 derived 79% of its revenue from recurring sources, down 1% from Q3 2024’s results.

i3's forward growth strategy consists of increasing its focus on the various submarkets within the public sector, more deeply integrating AI technologies into its product suite and making selective acquisitions.

Management believes that DOGE style cost-cutting may be starting to occur at certain state and local levels, which it views as a potential positive for its SaaS, user fee plus payment revenue model approach, but it is too early to tell.

Regarding valuation, the market is valuing IIIV at a forward EV/Sales multiple of around 3.5x on an estimated NTM organic revenue growth rate of 8% against a median Meritech Capital SaaS Index implied ARR growth rate of approximately 15% (Source).

Also, the benchmark index indicates a recent median Net Dollar Retention Rate of 110%. i3 has not disclosed its revenue retention rate performance.

The index of publicly held SaaS application software companies showed a forward EV/Revenue multiple median of around 5.0x on May 5, 2025, as the chart shows here:

So, by comparison, IIIV’s EV/Revenue multiple at 3.5x is currently a significant discount to the broader index average of 5.0x as of May 5, 2025.

i3 is facing headwinds from its Canadian business, and management has removed a $2.5 million revenue estimate from fiscal 2025 guidance.

Looking ahead, the company has improved its balance sheet with its recent divestitures and appears ready for potential future smaller tuck-in acquisitions as it focuses on various public sector submarkets at the state and local level.

But with topline organic revenue growth expected by management to be in the single-digits and no concrete plans for stock price support through buybacks, i3’s near-term future is a bit cloudy as public sector spending is under some degree of uncertainty that has yet to be quantified.

Given these uncertainties, my initial outlook on IIIV is a neutral Hold on lack of visible catalysts in the near term.

data.title: Upexi: The 'Strategy' Of Solana?
data.description:
By now, most in the financial world are likely aware of what Strategy (MSTR)(STRF) is doing with Bitcoin (BTC-USD). Through convertible notes, common stock issuance, and even preferred shares, Strategy is raising capital essentially any way it can in order to buy Bitcoin. What is less known, is that there are other companies attempting similar strategies with both Bitcoin and alternative digital assets. One such company is Upexi, Inc (NASDAQ:UPXI). Like Strategy, Upexi operates a legacy business outside of digital assets that is in clear decline. However unlike Strategy, Upexi is prioritizing Solana (SOL-USD) rather than BTC. In this article, we'll briefly look at Upexi's legacy business, examine the company's balance sheet strategy, and get into whether or not SOL is the right kind of asset for such a move.

Upexi's 'Re-commerce' Business & Bitcoin Mining

Prior to jumping headfirst in the world of crypto, Upexi primarily operated a 're-commerce' business where it sold wellness products direct-to-consumer and through Amazon's (AMZN) marketplace. Entities that were previously owned outright or partially by Upexi included Tytan Tiles, E-Core, VitaMedica, and Interactive Offers. Those assets have all been sold. The company still operates LuckyTail, PRAX, and Cure Mushrooms.

After significant revenue declines over the last several quarters and mounting net losses, the company found itself facing Nasdaq de-listing last November after failing to file both its fiscal 2024 10-K and subsequent 10-Q in a timely manner. Upexi regained compliance in late December. Prior to Nasdaq compliance issues, Upexi had already begun a restructuring that saw the company selling assets, brands, and eliminating debt. By the end of December 2024, the balance sheet had just $400k in cash/equivalents, $6.1 million in current assets, $4.2 million in net property, plant & equipment and $17.5 million in total assets. With $13.8 million in total liabilities - $10.6 million of which was current - and 1,045,429 shares outstanding at the end of the year, UPXI had a book value of about $3.60 per share.

This year, Upexi announced its shift in focus to Bitcoin and crypto through newly established and owned brands ChainBitMiner and QuantumHash. The former appears to simply be a holding company for Upexi's digital assets and the latter seems to be more mining-focused despite still being quite vague regarding the actual operations of the business:

The Company will look to capitalize with investments in Crypto mining with a main focus on Bitcoin mining. The Company does not initially plan to operate its own mining operations.

The quote above is from the Upexi's late-January press release. The quote below is current as of mid-May from the company's website:

Leveraging Hash-as-a-Service to mine our own coins, expand our digital portfolio, and create low-risk digital treasury levered growth. We seek to mine Bitcoin as well as high growth opportunity alt coins with immediate conversion to Bitcoin.

If I'm reading these accurately, it seems like Upexi's original plan was to scale BTC holdings through usage of cloud hash services similar to the ones offered by companies like Bitdeer (BTDR) and BitFuFu (FUFU). Which anyone who follows my work may know are often terrible deals for the mining plan buyers. In March, Upexi announced a letter of intent to buy a 2 MW Bitcoin mining facility but didn't provide much detail beyond the following:

The facility is currently utilizing less than half its available capacity output. Quantum Hash intends to update current equipment and add the latest and fastest mining machines available to maximize hash rate and maximize bitcoin mined monthly, upon closing.

As far as I can tell, active and prospective shareholders don't know where this mining facility is located or what kind of machines are currently hashing. More importantly, it appears as though the company has already pivoted priority again based on how it is allocating an enormous capital raise in April.

Solana Strategy

Less than two months after the company announced the Bitcoin mining LOI, Upexi raised over $100 million through private placement from several venture funds as well as prominent crypto advocates like Frank Chaparro, Arthur Hayes, Austin Federa, and Joey Krug. As part of this arrangement, the company issued 36 million common shares and has been deploying that capital into Solana purchases. After initially announcing the acquisition of 45,733 SOL for $6.7 million in late-April, Upexi subsequently added over a half million SOL in less than a month.

Announced Locked? Cost SOL Cost Basis
4/29/24 No $6,700,000 45,733 $147
5/6/24 No $23,216,705 155,767 $149
5/12/24 Yes $44,128,641 326,347 $135
N/A* No $10,154,654 68,867 $147
Total $84,200,000 596,714 $141

Source: Upexi, *Author's estimate based on Upexi cost basis and announced holdings

The table above shows announced and estimated purchases based on recent press releases. However, 596,714 SOL at a cost basis of $141 per coin is not an estimate; those figures were provided straight from Upexi in a May 12th press release. The company purchased over half its SOL stack at a discount because they're locked at a weighted maturity of 1.4 years. Meaning, after initially expressing interest in Bitcoin mining through both hash services and company-owned infrastructure, Upexi appears far more committed to the Solana ecosystem at this juncture.

So far, these acquisitions have actually worked out wonderfully. For $84.2 million, Upexi has acquired Solana tokens that have a current market value of over $104 million and the company is actively staking those assets for chain-native yield as well.

In Upexi's Spring investor deck, the company laid out its plan for accretive issuance through both common stock and convertible debt. The company specifically mentions in the deck how SOL ETFs and the return of memecoins are positive catalysts for SOL's price. I'm not generally a fan of memecoins and have expressed that in prior work. But if they are going to have a resurgence this year, Solana is likely to be the chain where it happens.

Is Solana The Right Play?

As of this article submission, Solana's reward rate from staking is 8.3%:

Keep in mind though that unlike Bitcoin, which is hard capped at 21 million coins, Solana doesn't have a limited supply. Instead, Solana has a 5.3% rate of inflation that declines over time. This gives Solana staking a 'real yield' of 3% before any appreciation or depreciation of the asset price. Looking at the 'real yield' on a quarterly average, we can see that the real yield line in purple has held up well in the 3-4% range despite nominal APY in general decline going back to Q4-22 - the declining inflation rate has supported real yield in spite of lower nominal returns.

Another consideration is fees from transactions. Like Bitcoin miners who make revenue from both issuance and transaction fee payments, Solana validators are paid through both token emissions and fees from network usage. The latter has been terrific for Solana since the start of 2024 as the network has grown share of fees among top smart contract blockchains:

Compared with some of the other top smart contract blockchains by market cap, Solana has generally fallen somewhere between 20-40% share of the market over the 8 months. Year to date, Solana has generated over $430 million in network fees which is second only to Tron (TRX-USD). And while one could certainly argue Solana's rally has been driven by the casino-culture that is memecoins rather than expansion of real utility through stablecoin usage, as long as memecoins have an incentive to exist, they will persist. Pump.fun - the interface used for memecoin generation - will be sharing 50% of Pumpswap revenue with coin creators in the future. This could perhaps drive more memecoins on Solana.

The Elephant In The Room

Keep in mind, UPXI did $4 million in revenue for the quarter ended December 2024 and generated a $1.3 million net loss in the same quarter. The underlying legacy business was worth about $3.60 per share at the end of December. With the $100 capital raise and nearly 40 million shares issued subsequent to the end of last year, we now have a company that likely has somewhere between $5 to 10 million in cash remaining after using $5.3 million of the $100 million capital raise for working capital and debt reduction as well as $84 million for SOL purchases.

So for a re-commerce business worth a few million at best, $10 million in cash at most, and $104 million in SOL that is earning a 3% real yield after inflation, investors can buy UPXI at a $373 million valuation - or nearly 4x the Solana held by the company. I doubt this needs to be said; but that's probably way too high a premium for exposure to staked SOL.

Closing Thoughts

Valuation aside, this is all quite the turnaround for an entity that was facing Nasdaq de-listing just a few months ago. Even the path to get here was a bit confusing. It looked like Upexi was making a play on raising capital to buy Bitcoin à la Strategy and Michael Saylor just a few weeks ago. However, it now appears as though Solana is the ecosystem Upexi is primarily banking on. Wise? Possibly. Provided the Solana can be staked for blockchain rewards, there's an argument to be made for acquiring SOL with the purpose of generating yield on those assets. Of course, things often come down to price. Even though Upexi may have purchased SOL at a great price, that doesn't mean $10 a share for UPXI is a good price. At a market cap just shy of $400 million and a little over $100 million in SOL - I'm going to go out on a limb here and call UPXI a 'sell.'

data.title: Tower Semiconductor Ltd. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Tower Semiconductor Ltd. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: SoftBank Group Earnings: All-In On AI, But At What Cost?
data.description:
For better or worse, SoftBank's (OTCPK:SFTBF)(OTCPK:SFTBY) Founder and CEO Masayoshi Son has decided to go all-in on artificial intelligence investments. That was made very clear when the company reported fiscal year 2024 earnings, which were positive for the first time in four years. One of the first slides drove home this point by saying that the SoftBank Group is, "Mobilizing the full strength of the Group to realize [Artificial Super Intelligence] ASI". As much as we believe in the transformative power of artificial intelligence, we do worry that the company might be investing at extreme valuations in some cases.

For example, the massive $40 billion announced investment in OpenAI, of which $30 billion is expected to be contributed by SoftBank and $10 billion syndicated, valuing the startup at an eye-watering ~$300 billion.

Perhaps more concerning for SoftBank Group investors, roughly 41% of its current net asset value (NAV) is attributed to its ARM (ARM) investment. We'll get into more details regarding ARM, but for now we'll just say we believe it is probably one of the most overvalued technology companies in the world. This is probably due in large part to the small float, as SoftBank has kept most of its shares. Further complicating things, SoftBank Group operates with some leverage, the LTV is ~20% which currently appears modest, but it might be much higher if some of SoftBank's assets are significantly overvalued.

Fiscal Year 2024 Earnings Results

SoftBank Group reported net income turning positive to JPY 1.15 trillion, around $8 billion, their first profitable year since 2020. Despite finally delivering net profitability, the company saw net asset value (NAV) decrease to JPY 25.7 trillion, roughly $180 billion, losing JPY 2.1 trillion compared to last year, close to $14 billion, reflecting unrealized portfolio value declines, such as ARM's lower share price.

The loan-to-value (LTV) ratio ballooned from 8.4% to 18% at the end of the year, and close to 20% pro-forma at the time of the report. The cash position also saw a significant reduction given SoftBank's significantly ramped-up investing.

ARM

One of the biggest risks we see with an investment in SoftBank is that more than 40% of its net asset value depends on its ARM investment. While there is no doubt that ARM is an innovative company with an attractive IP licensing business model, we believe it is probably one of the most overvalued large technology companies in the world.

For starters, we believe the company's competitive moat to be relatively weak. While it is convenient for many technology companies to simply license the ARM architecture, they appear willing to do so as long as the licensing and royalties remain low. It does not help that there are open source alternatives like RISC-V, which give customers leverage. A good example is that despite ARM's irritation with Apple (AAPL) for paying minuscule royalties, they ended extending the contract with them. There were reports that Apple was looking at the open source alternative RISC-V, which might have been a reason for ARM to capitulate.

[...] Apple has also reportedly looked into the open-standard RISC-V architecture (which wouldn't require any royalty payments at all) — potentially to give it leverage with Arm. We've seen Apple post jobs for RISC-V programmers before.

We have to give ARM credit for continuing to drive innovation, a good example being its high performing Armv9 based architecture for data centers and automotive applications, and a smaller version targeting Internet of Things (IoT) and edge devices.

ARM is therefore expanding its universe of opportunities, from mobile devices and data centers, to cars and smart homes. This is to say that we believe ARM is an innovative AI company, the main issue we have is the valuation at which it trades.

ARM looks expensive even when compared to some of the magnificent seven companies, with a forward Price/Earnings ratio almost twice that of NVIDIA (NVDA). This is despite the fact that analysts expect higher long-term earnings per share (EPS) growth for NVIDIA compared to ARM.

Another warning sign of overvaluation is the elevated percent of float that has been short, at more than 11%, this is more than 10x that of NVIDIA. With SoftBank Group holding almost 90% of ARM's shares, the price would probably experience a significant decline if the company started trimming its position.

OpenAI and the Stargate Project

We have a similar concern with respect to the OpenAI investment, which as we mentioned at the start was reportedly done at a ~$300 billion valuation. There is no doubt that OpenAI is one of the most innovative artificial intelligence companies in the world, the problem is a valuation of more than 20x expected revenue for 2025, when the company remains deeply unprofitable.

Similar to what we see with ARM, there are genuine reasons to get excited about OpenAI's prospects, which has seen a massive growth in active users. The problem, as is often the case when it comes to SoftBank, is the agreed valuation.

Even more concerning, SoftBank Group made a public commitment, together with OpenAI and Oracle (ORCL), to spend half a trillion dollars in the "Stargate Initiative".

Despite sounding like a sci-fi series, Stargate aims to make massive investments in AI Data Centers, AI Chips, and the required energy plants. In other words, it sounds like massive capital expenditures, at a time when some like Goldman Sachs (GS) are already concerned there might be AI infrastructure overspending, or as they put it, "Gen AI: too much spend, too little benefit?".

SoftBank Vision Funds

The problem of overpaying for investments is reflected in the poor performance of its VC investment vehicles, the SoftBank Vision Funds.

Their cumulative return since inception of -$475 is underwhelming, and this was despite the fact that many of its investments achieved public listings. To be fair, there are some investments that are likely to generate significant excitement if they IPO, including TikTok owner ByteDance (BDNCE) and mobile payments platform PayPay, which is used by roughly half the population of Japan and has been profitable for the past two years. Still, many startups are particularly sensitive to economic weakness, and cumulative returns for the Vision Funds have been disappointing. SoftBank Vision Fund 1 has delivered a $23.4 billion positive cumulative return, but this was almost negated by the negative cumulative return of -$22.9 billion for the SoftBank Vision Fund 2, while the Latin America and other funds added another cumulative $1.0 billion in losses.

LTV Leverage

One thing overvalued assets don't mix well with is leverage, and the loan-to-value for the SoftBank Group has been rapidly increasing. From less than 10% a year ago, it has been in a rapid uptrend and it currently sits at ~20.1%.

While SoftBank has committed to keeping this leverage ratio below 25% in "normal times", and below 35% during market distress, the risk is compounded by some assets being used for margin loans, which could force SoftBank to sell them at the worst possible time.

Mixed Track-record

Masayoshi Son is responsible for some of the best, and some of the worst, investment decisions in history. His reputation has been largely built on bold bets that grab headlines, bets that, amusingly enough, swing unpredictably between "brilliant foresight" and "what-on-earth-was-he-thinking?".

For example, he invested ~$20 million when Alibaba (BABA) was mostly an idea in Jack Ma's head. In fact, Masayoshi Son recounts that it was mostly the look in his eyes that convinced him to invest. This time his intuition was right and resulted in one of the most successful VC investments of all time, resulting in a more than 1,000x return. Masayoshi Son's intuition has not always been right though, perhaps the best example was the decision to invest billions in WeWork after a quick tour of one of its offices, and negotiating the investments during a car ride. Still, perhaps nothing sums up his investment style better than the exchange between OpenAI CEO Sam Altman and Masayoshi Son at a recent event.

[...] Son reminisced about past meetings with Altman. In 2017, Altman came to Tokyo looking for funding, but Son sent him away empty-handed. Two years later, as OpenAI developed one of the world’s most sophisticated AI models, Son offered to invest $1 billion in the venture. This time Altman refused.

Onstage, Son had a rosier recollection of the 2019 encounter: “You said that you’re going to go for AGI [artificial general intelligence]. I immediately said, ‘I believe you. I want to invest.’ From there I was a believer. I never doubted. Most people at that time thought you were crazy, right?”

“Some people think you’re crazy too,” Altman replied. “It all works out.”

Valuation

One reason we rate shares as a "Hold" is that the current valuation already discounts many of our concerns. SoftBank Group is trading at a significant discount to NAV, given its current market cap of ~$75 billion, which is less than half its net asset value.

We feel more comfortable with the valuations for the telecommunication investments, such as SoftBank Corp (OTCPK:SOBKY) and T-Mobile (TMUS), which tend to be stable, predictable, and cash-generative. We feel significantly less comfortable with the valuations for ARM and the Vision Fund companies, which tend to be risky startups still burning cash. Unfortunately ARM and the SoftBank Vision Funds represent ~70% of its NAV.

With ARM carrying a higher market capitalization compared to the SoftBank Group, the company might want to consider trimming its stake to increase the pace of share repurchases. This shows that either SoftBank Group is extremely undervalued, or ARM is extremely overvalued, but the current situation is confounding.

Using more traditional valuation metrics, such as the EV/EBITDA multiple, SoftBank Group is trading above its historical average. Still, the company has changed very significantly in the last few years, which is why we believe NAV is a better indicator. This is despite the belief that a discount to NAV is probably warranted.

Risks

The biggest risk we see for the SoftBank Group is potential asset overvaluation combined with leverage, including margin loans using investments like ARM as collateral. A potential recession and a tariff war would likely have a disproportionately negative effect on the types of companies SoftBank Group has been investing in, and could reduce their valuation in a very meaningful way. Take for example the recent Ampere acquisition for $6.5 billion, a startup that designs Arm-based server chips for cloud and AI. While it has significant potential, the company reported a loss of almost $600 million last year. Another important concern is the rapidly increasing net-debt.

Another metric signaling high risk is the low Altman Z-score, which is well below the 3.0 threshold, as well as its declining trend. Now, these risks are partially mitigated by the massive discount to net asset value at which SoftBank Group currently trades.

Conclusion

Despite its recent return to profitability, when looking more closely we continue to see a complex company with many of its assets trading at stretched valuations. The company is also overly exposed to recession risks with many of its Vision Fund bets still burning cash. If ARM and the group of Vision Fund startups made a small percentage of its assets we would be less concerned, however they represent most of the NAV. The discount to NAV mitigates these risks to a certain degree, but we prefer to simply avoid the stock. We would not bet against Masayoshi Son, but we won't be betting with him either.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: IWMY: May Perform Better Than Peers, Strategy Changes Ahead
data.description:
I have recently written about QQQY, an income ETF built around similar principles as the Defiance R2000 Enhanced Options & 0DTE Income ETF (NYSEARCA:IWMY). I strongly recommend reading the article on QQQY as a lot of the generics of how an important strategy change around the 27th of May 2025 (pivoting from ITM put writing to cash secured call spread) could change performance behaviors going forward, is covered in that analysis. You will also understand my general take on option writing income ETFs and their use cases.

I am writing separately on IWMY because the underlying is different. I have seen in the past that while the generic concerns and use cases remain mostly the same for all ETFs of the genre, some handpicked ones do perform better in use cases of riding the underlying better or even in risk adjusted returns. This article will talk about incremental points of note, taking off from the QQQY conclusions.

Volatility as a Key Indicator of Performance

A look at the volatility levels of the underlying is a first giveaway of predicting performance. Too low volatility leads to lower premiums on written options and accumulated income on wins are lower compared to few bigger losses on the downside. I am not discussing the perils of too high volatility because IWMY does not reach close to those levels (MSTR as a benchmark). In fact, the underlying (proxied as IWM), is well below what I usually call the lower cut-off - a stable ticker like Google. IWM is well below that threshold for continued option writing success, in theory. Even below the levels we saw for QQQ (underlying for QQQY) on average.

Performance of R2000 as an Underlying

Looking at past performance as a proxy of what to expect as the changes roll in for IWMY from the end of this month, I see the expected underperformance but there are green shoots.

First of all, the overall returns (total returns will include the impact of income payouts plus impact of capital change after payout) since inception is 13% vs 24% if invested in the underlying proxy IWM. That is a better performance gap than the 7% vs 29% I saw for QQQY vs QQQ. This is not significant yet, particularly given that point in time performance differences will vary. Plus, the tenure is not long enough to conclusively say that IWMY performs better than QQQY. But it is something positive to start with.

Secondly, the downside protection looks much better than some other income ETFs I have seen. Zooming in, the performance YTD (a falling market) shows a significant delta in favor of the income plan vs the underlying. That is, the underlying has fallen by ~10% including recent recoveries, where the IWMT total return is down by only ~4% in the same period. This downside protection augurs well for an income plan based on option writing - certainly performs better than a vast majority of option writing ETFs I have covered so far.

Investment Advice

My call on option-based income plans remains the same, i.e. simulate a payout matching IWMY (return of capital structured as systematic withdrawal) while holding IWM and you will most likely end up with better performance, particularly in the long term.

My call on IWMY specifically is a "Hold" anyway till the changes are effected, and we know better about the new structure and have some performance data to go with.

However, based on the underlying's behavior and the fund's past performance (although on a similar but different strategy), we can conclude which use cases suit IWMY best.

1. To generate income - Past performance ticks this box. That more income can be potentially derived by holding the underlying and paying yourself is known. But IWMY generates decent income (with lower underperformance to the underlying unlike some other ETFs, probably because the underlying is a broad-based index where large upside opportunity losses comes less into play than single stocks) at lower management hassles if you were to set up income yourself through IWM ownership.

2. To ride a sideways to downwards market - We have seen on the limited performance data available that IWMY should work better than IWM in such scenarios.

3. To ride the IWM in a less volatile way - I would say yes. Although conceptually, option riding strategies do not make the downside caps good enough to compensate for upside opportunity losses, IWMY's limited performance so far looks good in that regard. Probably because of the fact that the underlying is an index with limited shocks. Risk-adjusted returns look good for IWMY. If we look at performance from the start of 2024 (to view the lower volatility effects), we see how IWMY underperforms in bull rallies and overperforms in down markets. The reduction in volatility is not significant, but it is there.

4. To generate income to be deployed elsewhere - Payouts are significant. Investors who invested at inception would have received ~75% of the investments as payouts (at the cost of capital erosion, though). This is good for those looking at the payouts as a source of income diversion towards other investments. In other words, de-risking from R2000 at a good pace, while still retaining ownership.

data.title: Inflation Remained Tame In April But Fed Rate Cuts Still Appear Months Away At The Earliest
data.description:
By James Picerno

Investors were bracing for a clear sign of tariff-related pricing pressure in the April consumer inflation report, but consumer prices remained tame in Tuesday’s release. The Consumer Price Index (CPI) ticked down to a 2.3% year-over-year rate, the softest annual increase in over four years. The core reading of CPI (a more reliable measure of the trend) held steady, but the 2.8% increase vs. the year-ago level marks a steady pace at a four-year low.

Comparing the median Fed funds rate to CPI reminds that monetary policy remains tight and arguably overdue for cutting. Both headline and core CPI’s 1-year changes remain well below the central bank’s median 4.33% target rate.

The annual trend still looks encouraging in terms of approaching the Fed’s 2% inflation target, yet US central bankers remain cautious and prefer to delay cuts until there’s more support in the numbers for anticipating that inflation won’t rise due to tariffs in the months ahead.

Speaking on Monday, before the CPI data was released, Fed Governor Adriana Kugler said: “In the near term, higher import costs will raise prices for both consumer goods and inputs to production.”

No surprise, then, that the Fed funds futures market is now estimating that September 18 monetary policy meeting is the earliest date for a rate cut.

TMC Research’s multifactor Fed funds model continues to estimate that the neutral level for the target rate is still more than a percentage point below the actual median rate. That’s a sign that the Fed continues to keep policy moderately tight, presumably on the view that tariff-related inflation could heat up.

Although the published data opens the door for easing policy, the Fed remains worried that April is still too early to fully measure the effects of tariffs on prices. Last week, Fed Chairman Powell said: "We do not need to be in a hurry, and are well positioned to wait for greater clarity."

The notion that tariff-related inflation may still be brewing is partly based on the fact that the end game for raising import levies remains elusive. The April 2 “Liberation Day” tariffs that the White House announced are on pause, while the weekend deal between the US and China reached to lower tariffs didn’t offer a final agreement.

Meantime, US levies are still well above the level that prevailed prior to President Trump’s inauguration, according to estimates by the Budget Lab at Yale. “The overall US effective tariff rate has increased sharply to 17.8%, the highest since 1934,” the research shop wrote on Monday.

It's encouraging that several market-based estimates of future inflation have yet to break out of the recent range. One of Fed Governor Chris Waller’s favored measures of implied inflation continues to suggest that expectations haven’t changed much in recent history. The 5-year breakeven inflation rate (nominal 5-year yield less its 5-year counterpart) was 2.35% on May 12 – close to the Fed’s 2% inflation target, and well down from recent levels.

At some point, if the inflation trend holds steady or moves down, the Fed will probably cut rates. The pressure for easing will strengthen if the economy slows, although the numbers to date suggest otherwise. For example, the Dallas Fed’s Weekly Economic Index through May 3 continued to reflect a solid 2.3% four-quarter change in gross domestic product (GDP), which suggests that the Fed can keep rates steady for the near term without fear of derailing growth.

An upward shift in inflation could change the calculus, and perhaps quickly. A possible catalyst: CPI data for May, when data will more fully reflect the influence of tariffs. Meantime, the status quo prevails.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: AMLP: Understanding The Structure And Suitability Of This Income ETF
data.description:
The Alerian MLP ETF (NYSEARCA:AMLP) is an exchange-traded index fund offered by SS&C ALPS Advisors that is designed to track the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index. The Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index is a specialized index that tracks energy infrastructure companies that are structured as master limited partnerships. There are relatively few companies that fall into this category, and as such, the index only includes thirteen companies. This low number of constituent companies is a result of two crises that struck the energy industry during the decade following the 2009 Great Recession. The first of these crises was a 2014 collapse in oil prices. The second crisis was the COVID-19 pandemic, which also caused a temporary collapse in energy prices. During these two events, many master limited partnerships found themselves having difficulty obtaining capital and they ended up restricting their businesses as corporations, in the case of Kinder Morgan (KMI) and Targa Resources (TRGP), or merging with larger midstream companies, as was the case with Crestwood Equity Partners and Energy Transfer (ET) in 2023. These restructuring and acquisition activities have reduced the number of companies that are eligible for inclusion in the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index over the years, and as mentioned, there are now only thirteen.

There is another index from Alerian that is very similar to the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index. This other index is called the “Alerian MLP Index,” and it tracks seventeen companies. Despite the name of the fund suggesting that it might track this other index, that is not the case. The website for the Alerian MLP ETF specifically states (my emphasis):

The Alerian MLP ETF seeks investment results that correspond (before fees and expenses) generally to the price and yield performance of its underlying index, the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index(AMZI).

The website for the Alerian MLP ETF also includes this handy description of the fund:

Once again, this confirms that the Alerian MLP ETF is tracking the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index and not the Alerian MLP Index.

The Alerian MLP Index includes four more companies than the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index, but the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index still contains about 95% of the Alerian MLP Index. The difference between the two comes from their methodology. Any company meeting the following criteria is included in the Alerian MLP Index:

  • Structured as a master limited partnership,
  • Derives more than 50% of its trailing twelve-month cash flow from midstream energy operations,
  • Has a market capitalization of at least $75 million.

On the other hand, the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index has the following criteria for inclusion:

  • Structured as a master limited partnership,
  • Derives more than 50% of its cash flow from midstream energy operations,
  • Had a median daily trading volume of at least $2 million over the past six months,
  • Paid a distribution for the past two quarters.

The last two bullet points here are the key differences. Rather than simply requiring a minimum market capitalization, the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index has minimum daily trading and distribution requirements that eliminate companies that are illiquid or financially weak. This should make the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index a better pick for most investors. After all, illiquid or financially weak companies are generally only suitable investments for investors with very high levels of risk tolerance and large enough portfolios not to be bothered by losses or an inability to access their money on demand. This does not describe most retirees or near-retirees looking to use their portfolios to support their needs.

Important Characteristics Of The Alerian MLP ETF

The Alerian MLP ETF is a fairly popular index fund for investors who are seeking a very high level of income from the assets in their portfolios. A big reason for this is that, historically, companies that are structured as master limited partnerships are among the highest-yielding companies in the market. For example, let us look at the yields of the largest holdings of the Alerian MLP ETF:

Company

% of Fund Assets

Market Yield

MPLX LP (MPLX)

12.46%

7.68%

Sunoco LP (SUN)

12.27%

6.68%

Enterprise Products Partners LP (EPD)

11.91%

7.15%

Western Midstream Partners LP (WES)

11.81%

10.21%

Energy Transfer LP (ET)

11.41%

8.29%

Plains All American Pipeline LP (PAA)

11.05%

9.37%

Hess Midstream LP (HESM)

9.54%

7.62%

Cheniere Energy Partners LP (CQP)

5.27%

5.50%

USA Compression Partners LP (USAC)

3.66%

9.07%

Genesis Energy LP (GEL)

3.29%

4.72%

Suburban Propane Partners LP (SPH)

2.79%

6.50%

Global Partners LP (GLP)

2.78%

5.90%

Delek Logistics Partners LP (DKL)

1.65%

11.42%

While the exact yields of these companies will vary based on market performance, we can expect that they will almost always be among the highest-yielding companies in the market. In fact, most master limited partnerships have historically had higher yields than junk bonds, which alone would make them quite attractive to most income investors. However, we should keep in mind that all of the securities held by the Alerian MLP ETF are common equities as opposed to fixed-income securities, and these two asset classes will go through economic cycles very differently. Equities generally have substantially higher upside potential than any fixed-income securities. Over the five-year period that ended on May 7, 2025, the Alerian MLP ETF has substantially outperformed the domestic junk bond index (JNK):

While this will not always be the case, it does certainly show that the Alerian MLP ETF could provide a very attractive alternative to fixed-income securities for anyone who is seeking income and the preservation of purchasing power over time. After all, historically equities have been better than debt at preserving the purchasing power of wealth in the face of inflation. Thus, a high-yielding equity fund such as the Alerian MLP ETF could offer a worthy investment proposition for retirees looking for both a high level of income and the long-term preservation of purchasing power that is needed over the term of a lengthy retirement.

As the companies that comprise the Alerian MLP ETF have very high yields, we might expect the fund to have one too. This is the case as the fund yields 8.07% as of the time of writing. Historically, it usually trades with a distribution yield of around 7% to 9%, depending on the market level of the index. Thus, we can see that it is a bit better than most fixed-income index exchange-traded funds in terms of yield during most market conditions.

One of the big problems with the Alerian MLP ETF, unfortunately, is that it has a fairly large tracking error. According to Investopedia:

Tracking error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark.

In the case of an exchange-traded passive index fund like the Alerian MLP ETF, this refers specifically to how closely it tracks the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index. In this case, the fund is not even close to the index. The fund’s website provides this chart of its annualized returns over various periods compared to those of the index that it tracks:

Please remember that in this case, the fund is tracking the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index, so its performance against that index is more important than its performance against the Alerian MLP Index. We can immediately see noticeable differences between the Alerian MLP ETF and its benchmark in every single period, with most of them showing at least a few percentage points of difference. In fact, the Alerian MLP ETF has delivered a 5.28% total annualized return on net asset value since its inception date of August 25, 2010. The Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index, on the other hand, has delivered a 7.84% total annualized return over the same period. That is a pretty big difference, as most index exchange-traded funds are able to get somewhat closer to matching their indices.

The biggest reason for this is that the Alerian MLP ETF has more than 25% of its assets invested in master limited partnerships, so tax laws require that it be structured as a C-corporation rather than as a pass-through entity like most funds. This means that the Alerian MLP ETF suffers a drag on its performance due to taxes. As Yahoo! Finance explains:

AMLP accrues deferred tax liability for its future tax liability on the capital appreciation of its investments, and the distributions it receives on equity securities of MLPs are considered a return of capital. The deferred income tax expense (or benefit) represents an estimate of AMLPs potential tax expense (or benefit) if it were to recognize the unrealized gains (or losses) in the portfolio. All realized and unrealized gains (or losses) on investments and expenses may vary greatly from year to year and from day to day, depending on the nature of the fund’s investments, the performance of those investments, and general market conditions.

The majority of exchange-traded funds simply pass the tax liabilities on to their investors and so do not have to pay taxes on a fund level. This allows them to more closely track their indices as they do not have the performance drag that comes from taxes. Unfortunately, tax laws prevent funds that have a significant proportion of their assets invested in master limited partnerships from doing this, and this is one reason why exchange-traded notes are usually a better option for investors who are looking to track master limited partnership indices. Such notes are much rarer today than they used to be, though, which is at least partially due to the two industry crises that were discussed in the introduction.

The fact that the Alerian MLP ETF is structured as a C-corporation does allow it to be used by retirement investors, which could be a benefit. In some cases, it can create tax problems if an investor puts a master limited partnership in a tax-advantaged retirement account such as an individual retirement account. As Fidelity Investments explains, master limited partnerships can cause a retirement account to generate current-year taxable income in some situations. Basically, an investor would have to pay taxes even though the distributions paid out by the master limited partnership were received in the retirement account and might not be withdrawn for many years in the future. This is different from dividends received by a corporation, which do not generate current-year taxation if the common stock is held in a retirement account. The Alerian MLP ETF, since it is structured as a corporation, also does not have this problem. The taxes are handled on a fund level, so investors holding shares of the fund in a retirement account will not have to pay current-year taxes on the fund’s distributions. The tax rules for this fund work exactly the same as any other corporation. Thus, the fund provides a way for investors to include master limited partnerships in their retirement accounts without tax complications.

Partially due to the fund itself being subject to taxes, the Alerian MLP ETF has a higher expense ratio than most passive index funds. The website states that its expense ratio is 0.85%:

Let us compare this to a few other popular passive exchange-traded index funds:

Fund Name

Expense Ratio

Alerian MLP ETF

0.85%

SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY)

0.0945%

Invesco QQQ ETF (QQQ)

0.2%

iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM)

0.19%

(all figures from the websites for the respective funds)

As we can see, the Alerian MLP ETF has a substantially higher expense ratio than other passive exchange-traded funds. As expenses are a drag on the fund’s ability to track the index, this is a contributing factor to its underperformance compared to the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index.

About Midstream Energy Companies

All of the companies that are included in the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index, and by extension in the Alerian MLP ETF, are midstream energy companies. Midstream companies typically begin handling the hydrocarbon resources after they have been removed from the ground and frequently stop handling them once they reach the refinery. There may be some exceptions to this, such as refined products pipeline operators usually being considered midstream firms, even though refineries are downstream companies, but for the most part this definition suffices. Midstream companies generally engage in activities such as transporting, storing, and marketing oil, natural gas, and natural gas liquids. Many midstream companies also engage in the processing of natural gas.

One of the defining characteristics of midstream companies is that they tend to conduct business under long-term contracts that provide them with very stable cash flow. For example, here is MPLX’s operating cash flow for each of the years over the 2015 to 2024 period:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

As we can see, the company’s operating cash flow never experienced a decline during that entire ten-year period. This is in spite of the fact that crude oil prices were all over the place during that period. In fact, even the 2020 COVID-19 oil price collapse did not cause its cash flow to decline. This shows the strength of the business models used by most of the companies that are included in the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index. After all, as we saw earlier, one of the requirements for inclusion in this index is that the majority of the company’s cash flow comes from midstream operations. For nearly all of the companies in the index, that figure is far more than just a 50% majority.

The general cash flow stability of most midstream companies is nice for income investors because it provides a great deal of support for the distributions that they pay out. As we saw earlier, most midstream master limited partnerships have among the highest yields in the industry, and as such, they tend to attract investors who are seeking income. The last thing that most income-seekers want is for a company to cut its distribution, so the generally stable cash flows should provide some confidence that the company will be able to financially sustain its distributions.

At this point, there may be some readers who point out that some master limited partnerships have cut in the past during times of stress despite their stable cash flows. For example, Energy Transfer cut its formerly reliable distribution during the fourth quarter of 2020 (the announcement was made in October 2020, although it was officially the third-quarter 2020 distribution that was the first one at the lower level):

However, this was not actually due to a substantial decline in the company’s cash flow. The November 2020 distribution was 50% lower than the previous one, which was paid in August of 2020). It kept the distribution at that level until early 2022, when the company started raising it again. Energy Transfer’s operating cash flow did not decline by 50% in 2020 compared to 2019 levels. It also did not suffer from a lower operating cash flow in 2021 compared to previous years:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

The company’s operating cash flow for the full-year 2020 period came in at $7.3610 billion, which was only an 8.63% decline over the full-year 2019 level. Furthermore, the company’s operating cash flow for the full-year 2021 period came in stronger than the full-year 2019 level. Clearly, Energy Transfer did not cut its distribution because of financial problems. Rather, the company appears to have cut its distribution in order to use the money that would have been used for the distribution to reduce its debt. Thus, this does not disprove the statement that the midstream master limited partnerships comprising the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index typically have stable cash flows. However, this does illustrate a risk that investors may face if another energy price collapse occurs in the future. Unlike real estate investment trusts, master limited partnerships are not required to distribute a specific proportion of their earnings or cash flow to investors under U.S. tax laws. Some of them do have this requirement in their partnership agreements, but the fact that tax laws do not require the company to make distributions does allow them to cut their distributions even when their cash flow does not decrease. Thus, despite the generally stable cash flow, there is always the possibility that they will cut their distributions for whatever reason that management chooses. It is rare for this to occur, though, due to the fact that a distribution cut is taken as a bad sign by the market and usually causes the unit price of a given master limited partnership to decrease. During periods of time in which traditional energy companies are struggling to obtain capital in the markets, there could be a number of master limited partnerships cutting their distributions all at once. This would probably force the Alerian MLP ETF to also cut its distribution to its shareholders and, by extension, reduce the income of anyone holding it. This is a risk that investors should keep in mind when owning this exchange-traded fund.

How Investments Are Chosen For The Alerian MLP ETF

As stated earlier in the article, the Alerian MLP ETF is a passively-managed exchange-traded index fund that merely tracks the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index. As such, its strategy is simply to buy the master limited partnerships that comprise the index in the same proportions as the index. As is the case with all exchange-traded funds, the Alerian MLP ETF is constantly issuing and destroying shares in response to investor demands. This is how it is able to keep its share price relatively consistent with its net asset value per share.

Basically, the way that this works is that an “Authorized Participant” is able to purchase all of the securities that comprise the fund’s portfolio in equal proportion to what the fund itself holds, and then exchange them for newly-created shares of the fund directly from the fund sponsor. It is also able to do the reverse of this (purchasing shares of the fund on the open market and exchanging them with the fund’s sponsor in exchange for the underlying securities from the fund). The fund’s shares that the Authorized Participant provides are cancelled whenever this occurs. The Authorized Participant is essentially arbitraging the fund’s shares and the underlying securities anytime that there is a significant difference between the fund’s market price and its net asset value. In so doing, any difference between the fund’s shares and its net asset value is kept minimal because Authorized Participants will be tempted by the risk-free money to arbitrage away even tiny differences.

The fund’s prospectus states that at least 90% of its portfolio will include the securities in the index, and since it is trying to track the index as closely as possible, it is fair to assume that the weighting of every security in the fund will be pretty close to that of the index. Thus, we should have a look at how the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index determines weighting. In short, it is a bit complicated. First, the index is market cap-weighted just like the S&P 500 Index. The higher a given master limited partnership’s market capitalization, the larger its weighting in the index. Also, just like the S&P 500 Index, the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index is float-adjusted, so only the partnership units that are available for public trading are included in the calculation.

Where the methodology for the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index differs from the S&P 500 Index is that it has a cap of 12% of the index. Any securities that have a weighting of over 12% whenever the weightings of the index are being recalculated have the excess proportionately distributed to the other securities in the index that have a weighting of less than 12%. This process keeps getting repeated until everything in the index is at 12% or less of the index. As there are thirteen partnerships in the index, it is possible to get all securities at 12% or less weighting.

As mentioned earlier, only master limited partnerships with more than 50% of their cash flow from midstream energy operations, with a median trading volume of $2 million per day, and that have paid distributions for the preceding six-month period are eligible for inclusion.

The index is rebalanced following the above weighting process every quarter on the third Friday of March, June, September, and December. It is not rebalanced outside of these dates, so it is possible for a given company to exceed the 12% weighting on any given date if it outperforms the other companies in the index sufficiently.

It is worth noting that the market capitalizations of the constituent companies are not as of the rebalancing date. Rather, the index designers use the market capitalizations as of the Thursday immediately before the second Friday of March, June, September, and December. Thus, the market capitalizations used for calculating the weightings for the constituents in the index are about a week or so old on the day that the index is rebalanced.

The Alerian MLP ETF only has an 18% annual turnover, so presumably it changes its portfolio whenever the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index undergoes its quarterly rebalancing.

AMLP Peers And Other Options

The Alerian MLP ETF is the only exchange-traded fund on the market that tracks the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index. There is an exchange-traded note available that also tries to track the index, however. This security is the UBS ETRACS Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index ETN Series B (MLPB). As noted earlier in this article, exchange-traded notes can more accurately track master limited partnerships than any exchange-traded fund can because it does not have the drag on their performance from taxes. We can see this pretty easily in the performance of these two securities, as the exchange-traded note has outperformed the Alerian MLP ETF on a total return basis over the past five years:

As a note, the UBS ETRACS Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index ETN Series B is a debt security of UBS (UBS), and as such, it has default risk. UBS is simply paying the holders of this debt security whatever the return of the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index happens to be. This security’s payments are taxed at ordinary income rates for American investors, which could quite possibly be a higher rate than what that same individual would pay on the distributions of the Alerian MLP ETF. This issue could be overcome by putting the note into a retirement account, though. For investors who are looking to get exposure to the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index in their retirement accounts, the UBS ETRACS Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index ETN Series B is probably a better option than the Alerian MLP ETF, if that investor trusts UBS not to default.

There are a few other passive exchange-traded index funds that investors might alternatively choose to gain exposure to master limited partnerships. However, they do not track the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index:

Fund Name

Index Tracked

Global X MLP & Energy Infrastructure ETF (MLPX)

Solactive MLP & Energy Infrastructure Index

Global X MLP ETF (MLPA)

Solactive MLP Infrastructure Index

The indices tracked by these funds are different from the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index, although they might include different companies. The Solactive MLP & Energy Infrastructure Index, for example, consists mostly of midstream energy corporations, not master limited partnerships. Midstream corporations tend to perform somewhat differently from master limited partnerships in the market, and usually have lower yields than master limited partnerships.

As these two funds track different indices, most of the above comments regarding the composition of their portfolios and the methodology used to construct their benchmark indices are not relevant. Thus, the two Global X funds are not directly comparable to the Alerian MLP ETF.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Alerian MLP ETF is one of the more well-known and popular ways for investors to passively track an index of high-yielding midstream master limited partnerships. It has its drawbacks, however, most of which come from U.S. tax laws. Due to these laws, the fund is forced to be structured as a C-corporation and pay taxes on the fund level, which is different from most other passive exchange-traded index funds. This introduces a great deal of tracking error, and this fund consistently underperforms its benchmark index. Investors who are looking to track the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index in a retirement account would probably be better off with an exchange-traded note, but that does require a certain amount of trust in the financial strength of the issuer. Overall, this is not a bad fund as long as one understands its limitations.

This article answers these three main questions about AMLP:

  1. What types of investors is AMLP suitable for?
  2. What are the drawbacks to investing in AMLP?
  3. Does AMLP provide a higher yield than most ETFs?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for education purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.


Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
